news_summary: Today’s human rights progress includes calls for stronger African representation globally, advancements in protections for Indonesian domestic workers, and increased transparency in UK government use of AI.
news_summary_fr: Parmi les avancées enregistrées aujourd'hui en matière de droits de l'homme, on peut citer les appels en faveur d'une représentation africaine plus forte sur la scène internationale, les progrès réalisés dans la protection des travailleurs de maison indonésiens et une plus grande transparence dans l'utilisation de l'IA par le gouvernement britannique.
news_summary_es: Los avances de hoy en materia de derechos humanos incluyen llamamientos para una mayor representación africana a nivel mundial, mejoras en la protección de los trabajadores domésticos indonesios y una mayor transparencia en el uso de la inteligencia artificial por parte del Gobierno del Reino Unido.

articles.title: In Addis Ababa, Guterres urges reforms to give Africa stronger global voice
articles.summary: Africa’s rising influence is being constrained by outdated global institutions, unfair borrowing costs and cascading global crises, UN Secretary‑General António Guterres warned in Addis Ababa, as the United Nations and African Union reaffirmed their strategic partnership.
articles.content:
Meeting with AU Commission Chairperson Mahmoud Ali Youssouf, the two sides focused on deepening cooperation across peace and security, sustainable development and human rights.

It also marked what Mr. Guterres said would most likely be his final AU-UN summit as Secretary-General.

“This is my final AU-UN Conference as Secretary-General,” he told reporters in the Ethiopian capital afterwards. “But the ties between our organizations are stronger than ever – and will remain so.”

The Secretary-General and the AU Commission Chairperson signed a declaration reaffirming the principles of the UN-AU joint frameworks, further institutionalizing cooperation between the two organizations.

Pillar of global progress

Mr. Guterres described the African Union as “the flagship for multilateralism in Africa” and “a collective voice of justice for the developing world,” highlighting the continent’s importance and potential.

He said the Pact for the Future, adopted in 2024, underscored the importance of regional organizations, particularly the AU, and called for reform of the Security Council to make it more representative of today’s world.

“Africa’s continued exclusion from permanent representation is indeed a historical injustice – and we cannot accept it,” he said.

“This is not about privilege or symbolism. This is about ensuring that the Council is fit for purpose and able to act with legitimacy and effectiveness.”

Financial reform

The Secretary-General also highlighted what he described as deep inequalities in the international financial architecture, saying African countries continue to face high borrowing costs and limited access to affordable capital.

“Today’s global financial architecture is not providing adequate levels of support, nor sufficient voice and participation for Africa,” he said.

He noted that many African countries often pay up to three times the benchmark rate for borrowing, draining public resources that could otherwise be invested in water, sanitation, health and education.

Mr. Guterres praised African-led reform efforts, including the African Development Bank’s initiative to create a New African Financial Architecture for Development, bringing together African financial institutions.

“Africa assumes the leadership in solving African problems,” he said.

© African Union

Climate and energy

Mr. Guterres also warned that climate change continues to hit Africa hardest, even though the continent contributes least to the crisis.

A temporary overshoot of the 1.5°C temperature limit is now “inevitable – but not irreversible,” he said, calling for stronger adaptation finance and greater investment in Africa’s renewable energy potential.

By 2040, he said, Africa could generate 10 times more electricity than it needs entirely from renewables, while delivering power to the 600 million Africans who still lack electricity.

But he warned that Africa receives only two per cent of global clean energy investment, urging action to remove barriers such as weak grids, high capital costs and fragile supply chains.

Middle East fallout

In a separate meeting with French President Emmanuel Macron and the AU Commission Chair, Mr. Guterres also warned that Africa is especially vulnerable to the global economic effects of the Middle East crisis, particularly disruptions linked to the Strait of Hormuz.

He said even an immediate reopening would still bring serious consequences, including slower growth and higher inflation, while prolonged disruption could raise the risk of a global recession.

The effects, he warned, would be particularly severe for African economies already facing high debt, energy costs and difficulties accessing fertilizers.

Silencing the guns

Peace and security also featured prominently in the AU-UN talks.

Mr. Guterres said the UN strongly supports the African Union’s ‘Silencing the Guns’ initiative and the African Humanitarian Agency, while pointing to cooperation on conflicts in Sudan, South Sudan, the Democratic Republic of the Congo, Libya and the Sahel.

He called for an immediate cessation of hostilities in Sudan and renewed dialogue in the Sahel to confront violent extremism and terrorism.

“I have always believed that Africa should be seen for what it is: a continent of immense richness and promise,” he said.
articles.source: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
articles.reasoning: The article highlights significant efforts by the UN and African Union to reform global institutions for fairer representation and financial access for Africa, addressing historical injustices and promoting sustainable development, peace, and climate resilience. These reforms and partnerships have broad positive implications for millions across the continent and reflect meaningful progress in global equity and cooperation.
articles.personality_title: UN and African Union push for reforms to boost Africa’s global role
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Africa is growing in importance around the world, but old global systems and high borrowing costs hold the continent back. UN Secretary-General António Guterres met with African Union (AU) leaders in Addis Ababa to discuss ways to strengthen cooperation and give Africa a stronger voice globally.

**What happened** – During the meeting in May 2024, Mr. Guterres and AU Commission Chairperson Mahmoud Ali Youssouf signed a declaration to deepen their partnership on peace, development, and human rights. The UN chief called for reform of the UN Security Council to include permanent African representation and highlighted financial challenges African countries face, like paying high interest on loans.

**Impact** – This meeting showed a clear push to correct long-standing unfairness in global decision-making and finance. For example, African countries often pay three times more than other nations to borrow money, which limits their ability to invest in schools, hospitals, and clean water. The partnership also supports Africa’s efforts to fight climate change and increase renewable energy, aiming to provide electricity to 600 million people without power.

**What’s next step** – The UN and AU will continue working together on reforms, including pushing for changes in the Security Council and supporting African-led financial initiatives. They will focus on peace efforts in conflict zones like Sudan and the Sahel region, and work to attract more investment in Africa’s clean energy projects.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The UN and African Union are joining forces to reform global systems and finance, aiming to give Africa a fairer voice and more resources for peace, development, and climate action.
articles.personality_title_fr: L’ONU et l’Union africaine unies pour renforcer le rôle mondial de l’Afrique
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – L’Afrique prend de plus en plus d’importance dans le monde, mais des systèmes anciens et des coûts d’emprunt élevés freinent son développement. Le Secrétaire général de l’ONU, António Guterres, a rencontré les dirigeants de l’Union africaine (UA) à Addis-Abeba pour discuter d’un renforcement de la coopération et d’une voix plus forte pour l’Afrique à l’échelle mondiale.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Lors de cette réunion en mai 2024, M. Guterres et le président de la Commission de l’UA, Mahmoud Ali Youssouf, ont signé une déclaration pour approfondir leur partenariat sur la paix, le développement et les droits humains. Le chef de l’ONU a appelé à une réforme du Conseil de sécurité pour inclure une représentation permanente de l’Afrique et a souligné les difficultés financières des pays africains, comme les taux d’intérêt très élevés.

**Impact** – Cette rencontre montre une volonté claire de corriger les injustices anciennes dans la prise de décisions et le financement mondiaux. Par exemple, les pays africains paient souvent trois fois plus cher leurs emprunts, ce qui limite leurs investissements dans les écoles, les hôpitaux et l’eau potable. Le partenariat soutient aussi les efforts africains contre le changement climatique et pour développer les énergies renouvelables, avec pour objectif d’électrifier 600 millions de personnes sans accès à l’électricité.

**Prochaines étapes** – L’ONU et l’UA continueront de travailler ensemble sur les réformes, notamment pour changer le Conseil de sécurité et soutenir les initiatives financières africaines. Ils se concentreront sur la paix dans les zones de conflit comme le Soudan et le Sahel, et chercheront à attirer plus d’investissements dans les projets d’énergie propre en Afrique.

**Une phrase clé** – L’ONU et l’Union africaine unissent leurs forces pour réformer les systèmes mondiaux et le financement, afin d’offrir à l’Afrique une voix plus juste et plus de ressources pour la paix, le développement et le climat.
articles.personality_title_es: ONU y Unión Africana impulsan reformas para fortalecer el papel global de África
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – África está ganando importancia en el mundo, pero sistemas antiguos y altos costos de préstamo limitan su avance. El Secretario General de la ONU, António Guterres, se reunió con líderes de la Unión Africana (UA) en Addis Abeba para fortalecer la cooperación y dar a África una voz más fuerte a nivel mundial.

**Qué pasó** – En la reunión de mayo de 2024, Guterres y el presidente de la Comisión de la UA, Mahmoud Ali Youssouf, firmaron una declaración para profundizar su asociación en temas de paz, desarrollo y derechos humanos. El jefe de la ONU pidió reformar el Consejo de Seguridad para incluir representación permanente africana y destacó los problemas financieros que enfrentan los países africanos, como tasas de interés muy altas.

**Impacto** – Este encuentro mostró un claro esfuerzo por corregir injusticias históricas en la toma de decisiones y el financiamiento global. Por ejemplo, los países africanos a menudo pagan tres veces más por sus préstamos, lo que limita su capacidad para invertir en escuelas, hospitales y agua potable. La alianza también apoya los esfuerzos africanos para combatir el cambio climático y aumentar la energía renovable, con la meta de llevar electricidad a 600 millones de personas sin acceso.

**Próximos pasos** – La ONU y la UA continuarán trabajando juntos en reformas, incluyendo cambios en el Consejo de Seguridad y apoyo a iniciativas financieras lideradas por África. Se enfocarán en la paz en zonas de conflicto como Sudán y la región del Sahel, y buscarán atraer más inversiones a proyectos de energía limpia en África.

**Una frase clave** – La ONU y la Unión Africana unen fuerzas para reformar sistemas globales y financiamiento, buscando dar a África una voz más justa y más recursos para la paz, el desarrollo y el clima.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of two large, interlocking hands made of stylized African landscapes and UN symbols, rising together above a horizon where renewable energy icons like wind turbines and solar panels blend into peaceful doves soaring over a calm continent, all rendered in natural earth tones and soft blues.

articles.title: Indonesia Inches Towards Protections for Domestic Workers
articles.summary:
Click to expand Image
          



  
        
  

        
                      
      
        
              Activists demand the Indonesian parliament pass a bill to protect domestic workers outside the parliament building in Jakarta, August 14, 2023.
                    © 2023 Tatan Syuflana/AP Photo
          
    


  
Two female domestic workers, ages 15 and 18, jumped from the fourth floor of a Jakarta boarding house on April 22 in a desperate attempt to escape their employer. One died; the other was severely injured.The tragedy came just a day after Indonesia’s parliament finally approved the long‑awaited Domestic Workers Protection Law, granting domestic workers long denied legal safeguards. But passing the law is only the first step: the government now has one year to draft implementing regulations and set protection standards, slowing the rollout of critical safeguards.The two domestic workers were fleeing an allegedly exploitative environment—and they are far from alone. Indonesia has about 4.2 million domestic workers, 90 percent of whom are women. The International Labour Organization (ILO) estimated in 2012 that around 700,000 were children under age 18.Human Rights Watch has documented that domestic workers are often isolated in private homes, cut off from support networks, and trapped in conditions that heighten their vulnerability to abuse. Survivors of sexual violence reported feeling unable to escape, pressured to keep working for financial reasons, or threatened with further harm if they spoke out.According to Jala PRT, Indonesia’s National Network for Domestic Workers, there were 3,300 reported cases of violence and abuse against domestic workers between 2017 and 2024, including sexual, physical, and economic abuse, as well as forced labor, trafficking, and torture.For over two decades, civil society groups and unions have pushed for stronger protections. The new law, 22 years in the making, finally recognizes domestic workers as formal workers, regulates their conditions, and provides access to vocational training, health insurance, unemployment benefits, time off, and pensions. It also sets 18 as the minimum age for domestic work, banning the hiring of children who face greater risk of exploitation.“The most important thing now is recognition of working hours, religious holiday allowances, wages, days off, accommodation and food, as well as social security and social assistance—all of which have been missing and have kept domestic workers in poverty,” said Lita Anggraini, Jala PRT’s national coordinator.For the law to deliver real change, the government needs to implement it quickly, establish clear enforcement mechanisms, and ensure that domestic workers can safely report abuse. Indonesia should move to ratify ILO Convention No. 189 on domestic workers and 190 on violence and harassment, aligning national protections with international labor standards.
articles.content:
Click to expand Image Activists demand the Indonesian parliament pass a bill to protect domestic workers outside the parliament building in Jakarta, August 14, 2023. © 2023 Tatan Syuflana/AP Photo

Two female domestic workers, ages 15 and 18, jumped from the fourth floor of a Jakarta boarding house on April 22 in a desperate attempt to escape their employer. One died; the other was severely injured.

The tragedy came just a day after Indonesia’s parliament finally approved the long‑awaited Domestic Workers Protection Law, granting domestic workers long denied legal safeguards. But passing the law is only the first step: the government now has one year to draft implementing regulations and set protection standards, slowing the rollout of critical safeguards.

The two domestic workers were fleeing an allegedly exploitative environment—and they are far from alone. Indonesia has about 4.2 million domestic workers, 90 percent of whom are women. The International Labour Organization (ILO) estimated in 2012 that around 700,000 were children under age 18.

Human Rights Watch has documented that domestic workers are often isolated in private homes, cut off from support networks, and trapped in conditions that heighten their vulnerability to abuse. Survivors of sexual violence reported feeling unable to escape, pressured to keep working for financial reasons, or threatened with further harm if they spoke out.

According to Jala PRT, Indonesia’s National Network for Domestic Workers, there were 3,300 reported cases of violence and abuse against domestic workers between 2017 and 2024, including sexual, physical, and economic abuse, as well as forced labor, trafficking, and torture.

For over two decades, civil society groups and unions have pushed for stronger protections. The new law, 22 years in the making, finally recognizes domestic workers as formal workers, regulates their conditions, and provides access to vocational training, health insurance, unemployment benefits, time off, and pensions. It also sets 18 as the minimum age for domestic work, banning the hiring of children who face greater risk of exploitation.

“The most important thing now is recognition of working hours, religious holiday allowances, wages, days off, accommodation and food, as well as social security and social assistance—all of which have been missing and have kept domestic workers in poverty,” said Lita Anggraini, Jala PRT’s national coordinator.

For the law to deliver real change, the government needs to implement it quickly, establish clear enforcement mechanisms, and ensure that domestic workers can safely report abuse. Indonesia should move to ratify ILO Convention No. 189 on domestic workers and 190 on violence and harassment, aligning national protections with international labor standards.
articles.reasoning: The article reports the significant legislative achievement of Indonesia passing a Domestic Workers Protection Law after 22 years of advocacy, which grants legal recognition and safeguards to millions of vulnerable domestic workers. This law addresses widespread abuse and exploitation, sets minimum working age, and provides access to benefits, representing a meaningful improvement in human rights and labor protections with broad societal impact. Although implementation is pending, the law's passage marks a major positive step.
articles.personality_title: Indonesia passes new law to protect domestic workers after decades of struggle
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Indonesia has about 4.2 million domestic workers, mostly women, many of whom face abuse and poor working conditions. For over 20 years, groups have pushed for legal protections to improve their safety and rights.

**What happened** – On April 21, 2024, Indonesia’s parliament approved the Domestic Workers Protection Law. This law officially recognizes domestic workers as formal employees and guarantees rights such as health insurance, fair wages, time off, and training. It also bans children under 18 from working as domestic helpers.

**Impact** – This law is important because it gives legal protection to millions of workers who were previously vulnerable to exploitation and abuse. For example, many domestic workers live isolated lives, making it hard to report mistreatment. The law aims to change that by setting clear rules and benefits that help improve their daily lives.

**What’s next step** – The government now has one year to create detailed rules to put the law into action. This includes setting standards and ways to enforce the law so workers can safely report abuse. Experts also suggest Indonesia should adopt international labor agreements to strengthen protections further.

**One-sentence takeaway** – After 22 years of effort, Indonesia has passed a law that finally offers legal rights and protections to millions of domestic workers, marking a significant step toward safer and fairer work conditions.
articles.personality_title_fr: L’Indonésie adopte une loi pour protéger les travailleurs domestiques après des décennies de lutte
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – L’Indonésie compte environ 4,2 millions de travailleurs domestiques, principalement des femmes, dont beaucoup subissent des abus et de mauvaises conditions de travail. Depuis plus de 20 ans, des groupes militent pour des protections légales afin d’améliorer leur sécurité et leurs droits.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le 21 avril 2024, le parlement indonésien a adopté la loi sur la protection des travailleurs domestiques. Cette loi reconnaît officiellement les travailleurs domestiques comme des employés formels et garantit des droits tels que l’assurance santé, un salaire équitable, des congés et une formation. Elle interdit aussi le travail domestique aux moins de 18 ans.

**Impact** – Cette loi est importante car elle offre une protection juridique à des millions de travailleurs auparavant vulnérables à l’exploitation et aux abus. Par exemple, de nombreux travailleurs domestiques vivent isolés, ce qui rend difficile la dénonciation des mauvais traitements. La loi vise à changer cela en fixant des règles claires et des avantages pour améliorer leur quotidien.

**Prochaine étape** – Le gouvernement dispose maintenant d’un an pour créer des règles détaillées afin de mettre la loi en œuvre. Cela inclut l’établissement de normes et de mécanismes pour faire respecter la loi, afin que les travailleurs puissent signaler les abus en toute sécurité. Des experts recommandent aussi à l’Indonésie d’adopter des conventions internationales du travail pour renforcer ces protections.

**Phrase clé** – Après 22 ans d’efforts, l’Indonésie a adopté une loi qui offre enfin des droits légaux et des protections à des millions de travailleurs domestiques, marquant une avancée importante vers des conditions de travail plus sûres et équitables.
articles.personality_title_es: Indonesia aprueba ley para proteger a trabajadores domésticos tras décadas de lucha
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Indonesia tiene alrededor de 4.2 millones de trabajadores domésticos, en su mayoría mujeres, muchas de las cuales enfrentan abusos y malas condiciones laborales. Durante más de 20 años, grupos han pedido protecciones legales para mejorar su seguridad y derechos.

**Qué pasó** – El 21 de abril de 2024, el parlamento de Indonesia aprobó la Ley de Protección para Trabajadores Domésticos. Esta ley reconoce oficialmente a los trabajadores domésticos como empleados formales y garantiza derechos como seguro de salud, salarios justos, tiempo libre y capacitación. También prohíbe el trabajo doméstico a menores de 18 años.

**Impacto** – Esta ley es importante porque brinda protección legal a millones de trabajadores que antes eran vulnerables a la explotación y al abuso. Por ejemplo, muchos trabajadores domésticos viven aislados, lo que dificulta denunciar malos tratos. La ley busca cambiar esto estableciendo reglas claras y beneficios para mejorar sus vidas diarias.

**Próximo paso** – El gobierno tiene ahora un año para crear reglas detalladas que permitan aplicar la ley. Esto incluye establecer estándares y formas de hacer cumplir la ley para que los trabajadores puedan denunciar abusos de forma segura. Expertos también sugieren que Indonesia adopte acuerdos laborales internacionales para fortalecer aún más las protecciones.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Después de 22 años de esfuerzo, Indonesia ha aprobado una ley que finalmente ofrece derechos legales y protecciones a millones de trabajadores domésticos, marcando un paso importante hacia condiciones laborales más seguras y justas.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of two delicate origami figures shaped like young women standing side by side on the edge of a softly glowing rooftop, with gentle threads of light weaving around them symbolizing new protections and hope, set against the silhouette of Jakarta’s cityscape in muted, natural tones.

articles.title: New rules confirm public has a right to see how UK government uses AI
articles.summary: Government departments and other public bodies in the UK must consider requests to release information about AI-produced content, regulators have confirmed. The move follows a successful request by New Scientist for the release of a minister's ChatGPT logs
articles.content:
Government departments and other public bodies in the UK must consider requests to release information about AI-produced content, regulators have confirmed. The move follows a successful request by New Scientist for the release of a minister's ChatGPT logs

The use of AI chatbots is subject to the UK’s Freedom of Information laws Maurice from Zoetermeer (CC BY 2.0)

Text, images and other content produced by UK government departments and other public bodies using artificial intelligence are subject to freedom of information (FOI) laws, regulators have confirmed – potentially opening the door for the public to gain access to ministers’ ChatGPT or other chatbot records.

The Information Commissioner’s Office (ICO), the UK’s data-protection agency, has released new guidance confirming that “If staff at a public authority use AI for work purposes, the information generated will be subject to FOIA [the Freedom of Information Act] along with the prompts used”.

Last year, New Scientist successfully requested the then-UK tech secretary Peter Kyle’s ChatGPT logs under FOI legislation, in what is believed to be a world first. That triggered subsequent requests from other news outlets to obtain other information, but many have either been rejected on cost grounds or labelled as “vexatious”, an umbrella term that allows authorities to reject a request.

The clarification by the ICO could change that. “It will be very difficult for public authorities now to claim that AI-related requests are not subject to FOIA,” says Jon Baines at Mishcon de Reya, a London-based law firm.

“The ICO guidance is generally sensible, and won’t come as a surprise to most practitioners,” he says. “If information is ‘held’ in a recorded form by a public authority, wherever, and on whatever system, it is subject in principle to FOIA – so that must also apply to inputs to and outputs of AI systems and tools.”

Free newsletter Sign up to The Daily The latest on what’s new in science and why it matters each day. Sign up to newsletter

“I think it should be uncontroversial,” says Tim Turner, a data-protection expert based in Manchester, UK. “If records are captured by a public servant doing their job, they’re in scope,” he says. “That should be true for AI interactions and Post-it notes alike.”

The new guidelines could potentially enable successful requests for the prompts used by government workers in AI tools. The ICO has also suggested that public bodies may be required to use AI to summarise large documents or datasets when responding to requests, allowing them to answer requests previously dismissed on cost grounds.

Some have criticised the ability to obtain AI chat logs using FOI legislation. Matt Clifford, the chair of the UK’s Advanced Research and Invention Agency (ARIA), recently posted on social media that the ruling to release Kyle’s interactions with ChatGPT was “absurd”, “hugely corrosive and more or less guarantees that no minister will (say they) use AI”. Unusually for a public body, ARIA is exempt from FOI laws.

When asked if the new guidelines were triggered by New Scientist’s successful FOI request, the ICO declined to answer. A spokesperson says: “We regularly attend events and seek feedback on areas where both public authorities and requestors would value additional clarity and guidance. Our recent guidance on AI and FOI reflects what we have been hearing from organisations, and we tested the content with our external stakeholders to make sure it was as clear and useful as possible.”
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant policy clarification that government AI-generated content is subject to Freedom of Information laws, enhancing transparency and public access to AI usage by public bodies. This has broad societal impact by promoting government accountability and openness in the use of emerging technologies.
articles.personality_title: UK rules confirm public can access government AI content under Freedom of Information laws
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – In the UK, government departments and public bodies use artificial intelligence (AI) tools like chatbots to help with their work. Until now, it was unclear if the public could ask to see what these AI tools produce or the information entered into them.

**What happened** – The UK’s Information Commissioner’s Office (ICO), which protects data rights, has clarified that any content created by AI tools in government work is covered by the Freedom of Information Act (FOIA). This means people can request to see AI-generated texts, images, or logs, including inputs and outputs from tools like ChatGPT. This decision follows a successful request last year when the magazine New Scientist obtained ChatGPT logs from a UK minister.

**Impact** – This clarification makes it easier for the public to understand how government officials use AI, increasing transparency about new technologies in public service. It also means authorities can no longer refuse such requests simply by saying they are about AI. Additionally, the ICO suggests public bodies may use AI themselves to help summarize complex information when responding to requests, making it simpler and less costly to share data.

**What's next step** – With these new rules, more people and organizations might ask for government AI records, leading to greater openness. Public bodies will need to prepare to handle these requests and may begin applying AI tools to manage and summarize information. The ICO will continue to provide guidance to ensure clarity and fairness in this process.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The UK government must now treat AI-generated content as public information, allowing greater access and insight into how AI is used in official work.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le Royaume-Uni confirme que le public peut accéder aux contenus IA du gouvernement via la loi sur la liberté d'information
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Au Royaume-Uni, les départements gouvernementaux et organismes publics utilisent des outils d’intelligence artificielle (IA) comme les chatbots pour leur travail. Jusqu’à présent, il n’était pas clair si le public pouvait demander à voir les contenus produits par ces IA.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le Bureau du Commissaire à l’Information (ICO), qui protège les droits liés aux données, a clarifié que tout contenu créé par des outils IA dans le cadre du travail gouvernemental est couvert par la loi sur la liberté d’information (FOIA). Cela signifie que les citoyens peuvent demander à voir les textes, images ou journaux générés par l’IA, y compris les données entrées et produites par des outils comme ChatGPT. Cette décision fait suite à une demande réussie l’année dernière, lorsque le magazine New Scientist a obtenu les journaux ChatGPT d’un ministre britannique.

**Impact** – Cette clarification facilite la compréhension par le public de l’usage de l’IA par les autorités, renforçant ainsi la transparence sur les nouvelles technologies dans le service public. Les autorités ne peuvent plus refuser ces demandes sous prétexte qu’elles concernent l’IA. De plus, l’ICO suggère que les organismes publics peuvent utiliser eux-mêmes l’IA pour résumer des informations complexes lors de leurs réponses, rendant le partage de données plus simple et moins coûteux.

**Prochaine étape** – Avec ces nouvelles règles, davantage de personnes et d’organisations pourraient demander des documents liés à l’IA gouvernementale, ce qui conduira à plus d’ouverture. Les organismes publics devront se préparer à gérer ces demandes et pourront commencer à utiliser l’IA pour organiser et résumer l’information. L’ICO continuera à fournir des conseils pour assurer clarté et équité.

**Une phrase clé** – Le gouvernement britannique doit désormais considérer le contenu généré par l’IA comme information publique, offrant un meilleur accès et une meilleure compréhension de l’usage de l’IA dans le travail officiel.
articles.personality_title_es: El Reino Unido confirma que el público puede acceder a contenidos de IA del gobierno bajo la ley de libertad de información
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En el Reino Unido, los departamentos gubernamentales y organismos públicos usan herramientas de inteligencia artificial (IA) como chatbots para ayudar en su trabajo. Hasta ahora, no estaba claro si el público podía solicitar ver lo que estas IA producen o la información que se introduce en ellas.

**Qué pasó** – La Oficina del Comisionado de Información (ICO), que protege los derechos sobre datos, aclaró que cualquier contenido creado por herramientas de IA en el trabajo del gobierno está cubierto por la Ley de Libertad de Información (FOIA). Esto significa que las personas pueden pedir ver textos, imágenes o registros generados por IA, incluyendo las entradas y salidas de herramientas como ChatGPT. Esta decisión sigue a una solicitud exitosa el año pasado cuando la revista New Scientist obtuvo los registros de ChatGPT de un ministro británico.

**Impacto** – Esta aclaración facilita que el público entienda cómo los funcionarios usan la IA, aumentando la transparencia sobre nuevas tecnologías en el servicio público. También significa que las autoridades ya no pueden negar estas solicitudes solo porque se trate de IA. Además, la ICO sugiere que los organismos públicos pueden usar IA para ayudar a resumir información compleja al responder a solicitudes, haciendo más fácil y barato compartir datos.

**Próximo paso** – Con estas nuevas reglas, más personas y organizaciones podrían pedir registros de IA del gobierno, lo que llevará a mayor apertura. Los organismos públicos deberán prepararse para manejar estas solicitudes y podrían comenzar a usar IA para organizar y resumir información. La ICO seguirá brindando orientación para asegurar claridad y justicia.

**Frase para recordar** – El gobierno del Reino Unido debe tratar ahora el contenido generado por IA como información pública, permitiendo mayor acceso y comprensión de cómo se usa la IA en trabajos oficiales.
